title: Naked Single
description: When a cell has only ONE candidate left, that must be the answer!
concept: After eliminating all numbers that appear in the same row, column, and 3x3 box, if a cell has only ONE candidate remaining, that is the only possibility.

steps.text: Welcome to your first Sudoku lesson! 🎉 Look at the empty cells — each one shows tiny 'pencil marks' (candidates). These are all the numbers that COULD go in that cell.
A 'Naked Single' happens when all numbers except ONE have been eliminated from a cell. You can see it in the pencil marks — the cell has only one tiny number left!
Let's look at row 3 (highlighted in blue). It already has 3, 4, 6 filled in. The empty cells need: 1, 2, 5, 7, 8, 9.
Look at this cell's pencil marks. Check what numbers are already in its row, column, AND 3x3 box...
After checking row + column + box, most numbers are eliminated. If only ONE candidate remains, that's a Naked Single! 🎯
A Naked Single is the simplest technique — when only one number can possibly fit. As you solve more cells, more naked singles appear! ✅

title: Hidden Single
description: When a number can only go in ONE place within a row, column, or box.
concept: Even if a cell has multiple candidates, a specific number might be 'hidden' — it can only go in ONE cell within a row, column, or box.

steps.text: Great job on your White Belt! 🌟 A 'Hidden Single' is when a number can only go in ONE cell in a row/column/box, even though that cell has other candidates.
Look at row 1 (top row). It's completely empty! All 9 numbers need to be placed.
Check the first 3 cells — they're in box 1 (top-left). Column by column, which columns already have a 1 placed somewhere?
Column 3 has a 1 (row 4). Column 5 has a 1. So in row 1, 1 can't go in those columns. Keep eliminating!
After checking ALL columns and boxes, there's only ONE cell where 1 can go. The number is 'hidden' among many candidates! 🎯
Scan each row, column, and box asking: 'Where can number X go?' If only ONE place — that's a Hidden Single! ✅

title: Naked Pair
description: When two cells in the same group have the same two candidates, eliminate those numbers from other cells.
concept: If two cells in a row, column, or box have exactly the same two candidates, those two numbers MUST occupy those two cells. Remove them from all other cells in the group!

steps.text: Orange Belt time! 🧡 Naked Pairs let you eliminate candidates even when you can't place a number yet.
Look at row 1 — these two highlighted cells. They both have the same two candidates!
Since both cells can ONLY be 1 or 6, those numbers are LOCKED into these two cells. One is 1, the other is 6.
Since 1 and 6 are locked in the yellow cells, we can ELIMINATE them from all other cells in the same row!
These cells are ALSO in the same 3x3 box! Eliminate from the box too. 🎯
🎉 Find two cells with the same 2 candidates, eliminate those numbers from peers. Often creates new singles nearby!

title: Hidden Pair
description: When two numbers can only go in the same two cells, remove other candidates from those cells.
concept: If two numbers can only appear in the same two cells within a group, those cells can only contain those two numbers. Remove all OTHER candidates!

steps.text: Staying Orange! 🧡 Hidden Pairs are the opposite of Naked Pairs. We find two numbers that share the same two cells.
Check: 'Where can 6 go in this row?' And 'Where can 7 go?' If BOTH numbers can ONLY go in the same two cells — Hidden Pair!
Look at row 4. Many candidates. Let's check where numbers 6 and 7 can go.
Numbers 6 AND 7 can only appear in these two cells. No other cell in the row can hold 6 or 7!
Since 6 and 7 must occupy these two cells, remove ALL other candidates from them. The hidden pair is revealed!
🎉 Hidden Pairs clear extra candidates, often revealing singles! Scan for two numbers sharing only two cells.

title: Pointing Pair / Triple
description: When candidates in a box are aligned on one row or column, eliminate them from the rest of that line.
concept: If all instances of a number within a box fall on the same row or column, that number can't appear anywhere else on that row/column outside the box.

steps.text: Green Belt! 🟢 Pointing Pairs connect boxes to rows/columns. When all candidates of a number in a box line up on one row, they 'point' at eliminations outside.
In box 3 (top-right), the number 3 only appears in these two cells — both on the same row! The pair 'points' along the row.
Since 3 MUST be in one of the yellow cells, eliminate 3 from these other cells in the same row!
Same logic works for columns: if all candidates in a box line up on one column, eliminate from the rest of the column.
Pointing Triples work the same way — just three cells instead of two, all in the same box and aligned on one row or column.
🎉 Pointing Pairs bridge boxes and rows! Find a number constrained to one line in a box, then eliminate from the rest of that line.

title: Box/Line Reduction
description: When all candidates in a row/column fall in the same box, eliminate from the rest of the box.
concept: The reverse of Pointing Pairs! If all candidates of a number in a row or column fall within one box, eliminate that number from the rest of the box.

steps.text: Still Green! 🟢 Box/Line Reduction is the flip side of Pointing Pairs. The row points at the box this time.
Look at row 1. Check where the number 2 can go in this row.
All candidates for 2 in row 1 are in these two cells — both in box 2 (top-center)!
Since 2 must go in row 1 (in box 2), eliminate 2 from other cells in box 2!
The row 'locks' 2 into box 2. No other cell in box 2 needs 2. This often triggers a cascade of deductions!
🎉 Box/Line Reduction clears candidates from a box when a row or column constrains them. Combined with Pointing Pairs, these are your intersection tools!

title: Naked Triple
description: When three cells in a group share exactly three candidates total, eliminate those numbers from other cells.
concept: Like Naked Pairs but with three cells and three candidates. Each cell can have 2-3 of the numbers, but together they only use three numbers total.

steps.text: Blue Belt! 🔵 Naked Triples extend the Pair concept to three cells sharing three candidate numbers total.
Look at these three cells in the center row. They collectively contain only {5, 8, 9}.
A Naked Triple can be {5,8,9} {5,8} {5,9} — different subsets in each cell, but only three unique numbers total.
Since 5, 8, 9 are locked in these three cells, eliminate them from the rest of the row.
This often triggers a chain reaction — removing candidates here may create naked singles or pairs elsewhere!
🎉 Same logic as Pairs — three numbers locked in three cells. The key: only three unique candidates across all three cells total.

title: Hidden Triple
description: When three numbers can only go in three cells, remove other candidates from those cells.
concept: If three specific numbers can only appear in three cells within a group, remove all OTHER candidates from those cells. The triple was hidden by extra candidates.

steps.text: Still Blue! 🔵 Hidden Triples are trickier. We find three numbers that can only go in three cells, then strip away the disguise.
Look at row 1 — many candidates. Find three numbers that share exactly three cells.
Numbers {2, 5, 6} can only go in these three cells in row 1. Nowhere else! But each cell has other candidates too.
Since {2,5,6} must go in these cells, remove ALL other candidates from them!
After stripping away the extra candidates, the triple becomes a naked triple — and may lead to further eliminations.
🎉 Hidden Triples clear away disguise candidates, often leaving naked pairs or singles! Hard to spot but very rewarding.

title: X-Wing
description: When a number appears in exactly two cells in two rows, and those cells share the same two columns.
concept: An X-Wing forms a rectangle: a number appears in exactly 2 cells in each of 2 rows, and those cells are in the same 2 columns. Eliminate that number from the rest of those columns!

steps.text: Purple Belt! 🟣 X-Wing is your first 'fish' pattern — looking at a single number across the ENTIRE board.
An X-Wing forms when a candidate appears in exactly 2 cells in each of 2 rows, and those cells share the same 2 columns — forming a rectangle.
In one row, the number 7 (check pencil marks) can only go in these two cells. They're in columns 2 and 8.
In another row, 7 also appears in exactly two cells — in the SAME columns 2 and 8! We have a rectangle of 7s.
The four corners form an X-Wing! Since 7 must go in diagonally opposite corners, eliminate 7 from other cells in those columns.
🎉 X-Wing is the gateway to fish patterns! The same logic works with columns → rows. Look for rectangles of a single candidate.

title: Swordfish
description: Like X-Wing but with three rows and three columns — the bigger fish!
concept: Swordfish extends X-Wing to 3 rows/columns. If a candidate appears in exactly 2-3 cells in each of 3 rows, and all candidates lie in just 3 columns, eliminate from the rest of those columns.

steps.text: Still Purple! 🟣 Swordfish is X-Wing's bigger sibling. Instead of 2 rows × 2 columns, it uses 3 rows × 3 columns.
If a candidate appears in 2-3 cells in each of 3 rows, and ALL those candidates fall within just 3 columns — that's a Swordfish!
This is the famous 'World's Hardest Sudoku' by Arto Inkala. It needs advanced techniques like Swordfish to solve.
Look for a number that's constrained to just 3 columns across 3 different rows. The candidates form a fish-like shape.
Eliminate that candidate from the rest of those 3 columns. The pattern is harder to spot than X-Wing but follows the same logic!
🎉 Swordfish, X-Wing, and Jellyfish (4×4) are all fish patterns. The bigger the fish, the harder to spot — but the elimination power is the same!

title: XY-Wing (Y-Wing)
description: Three cells with candidates {AB}, {AC}, {BC} — eliminate C from cells that see both pincers.
concept: An XY-Wing uses three cells: a 'pivot' with {A,B}, and two 'pincers' with {A,C} and {B,C}. Whatever A or B the pivot is, C must appear in one of the pincers. Eliminate C from any cell that sees BOTH pincers.

steps.text: Brown Belt! 🟤 XY-Wing (also called Y-Wing) uses three cells with overlapping candidates to eliminate a number.
Find a 'pivot' cell with exactly 2 candidates {A,B}. Then find two 'pincer' cells — one with {A,C} and one with {B,C}. The pincers must see the pivot.
This highlighted cell is our pivot. It has exactly two candidates — say {A, B}.
These two cells are our pincers — one shares candidate A with the pivot, the other shares B. Both also contain a common candidate C.
No matter whether the pivot is A or B, one pincer will always be C. So any cell that sees BOTH pincers cannot be C!
🎉 XY-Wing is powerful! Find the pivot with 2 candidates, locate the pincers, then eliminate from cells seeing both pincers.

title: XYZ-Wing
description: Like XY-Wing but the pivot has three candidates — even more powerful!
concept: An XYZ-Wing has a pivot with {A,B,C} and two pincers: one with {A,C} and one with {B,C}. The shared candidate C can be eliminated from any cell that sees all three cells.

steps.text: Still Brown! 🟤 XYZ-Wing is XY-Wing's bigger cousin. The pivot now has THREE candidates instead of two.
The pivot has {A,B,C}. One pincer has {A,C}, the other {B,C}. Since the pivot must contain C in some form, C can be eliminated from cells that see ALL three.
The key difference from XY-Wing: the elimination target must see ALL THREE cells (pivot + both pincers), not just the pincers.
Look for a cell with 3 candidates where two of its peers each share exactly 2 of those 3 candidates.
This is one of the harder patterns to spot, but it's extremely satisfying when you find one!
🎉 XYZ-Wing extends XY-Wing logic. Master both and you have a powerful toolkit for eliminating candidates in tough puzzles!

title: Unique Rectangle
description: Avoid deadly patterns — if four cells form a rectangle with the same pair, one must be different.
concept: Valid Sudoku puzzles have exactly one solution. If four cells form a rectangle (2 rows × 2 cols, same 2 boxes) and all contain the same two candidates, that creates multiple solutions. One cell must have an extra candidate!

steps.text: Black Belt! ⬛ Unique Rectangles use a powerful meta-rule: every valid Sudoku has exactly ONE solution.
If four cells form a rectangle (2 rows, 2 columns, 2 boxes) and all contain the same pair {X,Y}, then X and Y could swap — TWO solutions! This is forbidden.
Look for four cells forming a rectangle. If three corners have the same two candidates and the fourth has those plus extras...
The extra candidates in the fourth cell break the deadly pattern! So that cell CANNOT be just {X,Y} — one of the extra candidates must be the answer.
We can eliminate {X,Y} from the fourth corner cell! Its extra candidates are the only valid options.
🎉 Unique Rectangles use Sudoku's one-solution rule to eliminate candidates. Look for rectangular patterns with matching pairs!

title: Simple Coloring
description: Color conjugate pairs to find contradictions and eliminate candidates.
concept: When a candidate appears in exactly two cells in a group (a conjugate pair), we can 'color' them alternately. If a color creates a contradiction (same color appears twice in a group), eliminate all cells of that color.

steps.text: Black Belt coloring! ⬛ When a number appears in only 2 cells in a group, those cells form a 'conjugate pair' — one is the answer, the other isn't.
Color one cell blue (ON) and the other red (OFF). Follow the chain — each conjugate cell gets the opposite color.
Start with this cell colored blue (ON) for a specific candidate number.
Follow the chain — this cell gets the opposite color (red/OFF). Keep following through more conjugate pairs.
If two cells of the same color appear in the same group, that's a contradiction! All cells with that color can be eliminated.
🎉 Coloring chains are powerful for hard puzzles. Color conjugate pairs alternately, find contradictions, and eliminate the impossible color!

title: W-Wing
description: Two cells with the same pair linked by a conjugate pair in a third group.
concept: A W-Wing finds two cells with the same two candidates {X,Y}. If they're connected by a conjugate pair of X (or Y) in a shared row/column/box, then Y (or X) can be eliminated from cells that see both endpoints.

steps.text: Last Black Belt technique! ⬛ W-Wing is a chain pattern that connects two cells through a third group.
Find two cells with the same pair {X,Y}. Check if they're connected by a conjugate pair of X in some row, column, or box.
If the conjugate pair guarantees that X appears in one of the two connecting positions, then Y must appear in the other.
This means Y can be eliminated from any cell that sees BOTH of the original {X,Y} cells.
W-Wings are like a simplified forcing chain — they prove a number must be in one of two places without following a long chain.
🎉 W-Wing completes your Black Belt toolkit! Combined with Coloring and Unique Rectangles, you can tackle the hardest puzzles ever created!
